definition: True corporate leverage is 5.5-7.5x vs reported 4.0-4.5x due to EBITDA addback practices, cross-validated by S&P LCD at 75-85% addback realization rates. Merton-model recalibration shifts 5-year cumulative default probability from ~3.5% to ~8-14%. The directional claim (spreads too tight vs adjusted fundamentals) is robust; the magnitude claim is uncertain.
evidence: S&P LCD cross-validation at 75-85% addback realization rates
Pair_1 debate: 'the single most empirically grounded finding across both analyses'
Directional claim survives robustly but 'negative risk premium' formulation refuted
Merton-model recalibration shifts 5Y default probability from ~3.5% to ~8-14%
open_questions: Do sophisticated HY investors independently haircut EBITDA partially pricing the distortion?
How much does the Kalecki floor reduce true default probability relative to Merton-model estimates?
What is the distribution of addback realization rates across sectors and credit quality?
